question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY to a syntactically valid but wrong private key so that cfg.operatorKey passes the non-empty check but causes all transactions submitted by submitWithRetry() to fail signature verification, generating a stream of failed transaction records in the mirror node history that reorganize the apparent activity pattern of the operator account?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK to 'other' and HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to a URL that returns a nodesEnvelope with a very large number of node entries (e.g., thousands), causing buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to build an oversized network map that causes the hiero SDK to distribute transactions across many nodes in an unpredictable order, reorganizing the per-node transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to a URL that intermittently returns different node lists on successive calls, and since buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() is only called once at startup (sdk_client.go line 18), the attacker can time the startup to coincide with a malicious response and permanently redirect all transactions for the lifetime of the pinger process?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/config.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK to 'other' and HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to a URL that returns a nodesEnvelope with ServiceEndpoints using IPv6 addresses, and since net.JoinHostPort() in mirror_node_client.go (line 122) handles IPv6 differently, exploit any parsing inconsistency to cause the SDK to connect to an unintended host and route transactions to attacker-controlled nodes?
